content.full_text:
California Legislature

Senate Legislative
Ethics Committee

MEMBERS
WILLIAM A. CRAVEN
VICE CHAIRMAN
MARIAN BERGESON
ED DAVIS
ROBERT PRESLEY
NICHOLAS PETRIS

ANN BAILEY
CHIEF COUNSEL

LEROY GREENE
Chairman

September 4, 1991

Scott Hallabrin
Acting General Counsel
Fair Political Practices Commission
428 J Street, Suite 800
P.O. Box 807
Sacramento, CA 95804

Re: Request for Advice

Dear Mr. Hallabrin:

On behalf of the Senate Committee on Legislative Ethics,
I am requesting advice regarding the disclosure and
valuation of airport parking passes provided to Members
of the Legislature.

These are passes which may be provided to Members by
commercial airports in California. (These airports are
all organized somewhat differently, but it is my
understanding that all are governmental agencies of some
sort or another). Typically the passes are used by
Members when commuting to Sacramento or other cities for
legislative business; they may, however, be used for
personal business as well.

I understand that use of the pass may not be subject to
disclosure when used in connection with official business
(see In re Thomas (1977), 3 FPPC Ops. 30; Glad FPPC
advice letter, your file No. I-90-148). This prior
advice appears to require that the pass be treated as a
reportable gift only to the extent that the recipient

STATE CAPITOL • ROOM 412 • SACRAMENTO, CALIFORNIA 95814 • (916) 324-6929

Scott Hallabrin
September 4, 1991
Page 2


Member of the Legislature actually uses the pass for
personal purposes and only if the value of this personal
use is $50 or more. Please confirm if this
interpretation of the law as it applies to airport
parking passes is correct. Proposed commission
regulations governing gifts do not appear to address this
issue.

If you have questions about this request, please contact
me at 324-6929. Thank you.

Sincerely,

[signature]
ANN BAILEY

AB:lc

sections.question: Please confirm if this interpretation of the law, as it applies to airport parking passes is correct: that use of the pass may not be subject to disclosure when used in connection with official business, and that the pass be treated as a reportable gift only to the extent that the recipient Member of the Legislature actually uses the pass for personal purposes and only if the value of this personal use is $50 or more.
sections.question_synthetic: Whether airport parking passes provided to California legislators must be disclosed and valued as gifts, and specifically whether such passes are reportable only when used for personal purposes and only if the personal use value exceeds $50.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: This is an incoming request letter from the Senate Legislative Ethics Committee to the FPPC, not an FPPC advice letter response. No formal QUESTION or CONCLUSION sections are present; the question is posed in narrative form within the body of the letter. The document references prior FPPC authority (In re Thomas and a Glad FPPC advice letter with file No. I-90-148) that the requester is seeking to have confirmed.

embedding.qa_text: QUESTION: Please confirm if this interpretation of the law, as it applies to airport parking passes is correct: that use of the pass may not be subject to disclosure when used in connection with official business, and that the pass be treated as a reportable gift only to the extent that the recipient Member of the Legislature actually uses the pass for personal purposes and only if the value of this personal use is $50 or more.

embedding.summary: The California Senate Committee on Legislative Ethics requests FPPC advice on the proper disclosure and valuation treatment of airport parking passes provided to Members of the Legislature, seeking confirmation that such passes are reportable gifts only when used for personal purposes with a value of $50 or more.
